enrichment.short_description: Bump.sh turns OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, and Arazzo specification files into always up-to-date, interactive API reference documentation and changelogs.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bump.sh generates interactive API docs from OpenAPI/AsyncAPI specs with automatic changelogs. See pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bump.sh is an API documentation platform, founded in 2020 by CEO Sebastien Charrier and headquartered in Angers, France. The company operates as a small, fully remote, France-based team. Bump.sh converts machine-readable API specification files - OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, and Arazzo - into hosted, always-current reference documentation using a docs-as-code workflow: teams push spec changes via CLI or GitHub/GitLab CI integration and Bump.sh renders an updated documentation portal automatically. Charrier conceived the product while rewriting the payment API at Capitaine Train, where he experienced firsthand how quickly hand-maintained API docs go stale. Distinguishing features include server-side rendering that can handle very large OpenAPI files without breaking, an automated changelog that detects and flags breaking changes between spec versions, an in-browser API Explorer with OAuth auto-fill so developers can test endpoints directly from the docs, and a Hubs feature that lets teams publish a single catalog page listing multiple related APIs. Bump.sh has also added MCP (Model Context Protocol) tooling so AI coding agents can query API documentation directly, positioning the product for both human and AI-agent consumption of API references. Pricing is subscription-based across three tiers. The Basic plan is $50/month and covers 10 API docs, 5 MCP tools, 3 internal users, and 20 guest users, with OpenAPI/AsyncAPI/Arazzo support, CLI and GitHub integration, and a custom domain. The Pro plan is $250/month (with a 14-day free trial) and raises limits to 30 docs, 50 MCP tools, 5 internal users, and 40 guests, adding the API Explorer, private MCP servers, branch management, full CI integration, release rollback, and automatic changelogs. A Custom/Enterprise tier offers unlimited users, SSO, a reverse proxy/embed mode, an on-premise MCP dataplane, custom reporting, and a 99.99% SLA, with pricing set through direct sales conversations. Bump.sh also gives verified open-source projects free access to the Pro plan. The product targets API-first engineering teams, developer relations teams, and platform teams who maintain public or partner-facing APIs and want documentation that cannot drift out of sync with the actual spec. It competes with tools like Redocly, Stoplight, ReadMe, and Swagger UI/Hub in the API documentation and developer portal space. Support is described by the company as full human support available on every plan, including the Basic tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Bump.sh is a docs-as-code API documentation platform that renders OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, and Arazzo specification files into hosted, interactive reference sites. Instead of manually writing and updating API documentation, teams commit their spec files and let Bump.sh regenerate the docs automatically whenever the spec changes.

The company was founded in 2020 by CEO Sebastien Charrier in Angers, France, after he experienced the pain of maintaining hand-written API docs while rewriting a payment API at a previous company. Bump.sh operates as a small, fully remote team based in France.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include server-side rendering built to handle very large OpenAPI files, an automated changelog that flags breaking changes between spec versions, an in-doc API Explorer with OAuth auto-fill for testing endpoints directly from the documentation, and Hubs for publishing a catalog of multiple related APIs under one portal.

Bump.sh also supports CLI and Git-based CI/CD integration (GitHub, GitLab) so documentation updates ship as part of the normal deployment pipeline, plus branch previews, release rollback, and Slack notifications on higher-tier plans. More recently the company added MCP (Model Context Protocol) tooling so AI coding agents can query API references programmatically.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bump.sh has no permanently free public tier; pricing starts at $50/month for the Basic plan (10 API docs, 3 internal users, 20 guests). The Pro plan is $250/month with a 14-day free trial and adds the API Explorer, private MCP servers, and full CI integration. An Enterprise/Custom plan adds SSO, an SLA, and unlimited users at custom pricing.
Verified open-source projects can apply for free access to the Pro plan, which is a notable exception to the otherwise fully paid pricing model.

features.title: Spec-driven documentation
features.description: Generates reference docs directly from OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, and Arazzo specification files, keeping docs in sync with the actual API contract.

features.title: Automatic changelog & breaking-change detection
features.description: Diffs each new spec version against the previous one and publishes a changelog that flags breaking changes automatically.

features.title: API Explorer
features.description: An in-documentation console with OAuth auto-fill lets developers send live requests against the API without leaving the docs page.

features.title: CI/CD and CLI integration
features.description: A CLI and native GitHub/GitLab integrations let teams publish documentation updates as part of their existing deployment pipeline.

features.title: API Hubs
features.description: Publishes a single catalog page that groups multiple related API documentation sites for larger API portfolios.

features.title: MCP tooling for AI agents
features.description: Exposes API documentation through Model Context Protocol servers so AI coding assistants can query API references directly.

features.title: Branch management & rollback
features.description: Pro and higher plans support documentation branches, previews, and one-click rollback to a prior release.

pros: Documentation stays automatically in sync with the OpenAPI/AsyncAPI source of truth
Automated breaking-change detection reduces surprises for API consumers
In-doc API Explorer lets users test endpoints without external tools
Free Pro-tier access available for verified open-source projects
cons: No permanently free public plan for commercial use
Pricing per API doc/user can add up for teams with many services
Smaller ecosystem and market presence than larger competitors like Redocly or ReadMe
Enterprise features (SSO, on-prem MCP dataplane) require custom sales pricing

faqs.question: What is Bump.sh used for?
faqs.answer: Bump.sh turns OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, and Arazzo specification files into hosted, interactive API reference documentation that updates automatically whenever the underlying spec changes.

faqs.question: Does Bump.sh have a free plan?
faqs.answer: There is no permanently free public plan, but verified open-source projects can apply for free access to the Pro plan, and paid plans start at $50/month.

faqs.question: Does Bump.sh support breaking-change detection?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bump.sh automatically diffs each new API spec version against the previous one and generates a changelog that highlights breaking changes.

faqs.question: Can Bump.sh integrate with CI/CD pipelines?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bump.sh offers a CLI and native GitHub/GitLab integrations so documentation deploys automatically as part of existing build pipelines.

faqs.question: Who founded Bump.sh?
faqs.answer: Bump.sh was founded in 2020 by CEO Sebastien Charrier in Angers, France.

use_cases.title: Public API reference documentation
use_cases.description: Teams publishing a public or partner-facing API use Bump.sh to keep reference docs automatically synced with the live API spec.

use_cases.title: Multi-API developer portals
use_cases.description: Organizations with several microservices or APIs use Hubs to present a unified documentation catalog to internal and external developers.

use_cases.title: AI-agent-readable API references
use_cases.description: Companies exposing MCP endpoints let AI coding assistants query API documentation programmatically rather than scraping HTML docs.

enrichment.short_description: Burp Suite is PortSwigger's web application security testing toolkit, combining an intercepting proxy, scanner, and manual testing tools for penetration testers.
enrichment.seo_title: Burp Suite Review 2026: Pricing, Features & Editions
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Burp Suite by PortSwigger is a leading web app security testing toolkit. Compare Community, Professional, and Enterprise editions, pricing, and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Burp Suite is a web application security testing toolkit built by PortSwigger, a company founded in 2008 by Dafydd Stuttard and headquartered in Knutsford, Cheshire, in the northwest of England. PortSwigger has grown into a market leader in web security testing tools, employing several hundred people (reported around 338 as of mid-2026) and receiving a $112 million growth investment from Brighton Park Capital in 2024. Burp Suite is used by an estimated 80,000+ individual security professionals and over 1,000 enterprises, including large organizations such as Microsoft, Amazon, and Salesforce, and it underpins much of PortSwigger's free Web Security Academy training content. The product ships in three editions. Burp Suite Community Edition is free and includes the core manual testing toolkit - an intercepting HTTP/WebSocket proxy, Repeater for resending and modifying individual requests, a throttled version of Intruder for automated attacks, and Burp's Decoder, Comparer, and Sequencer utilities - but omits the automated vulnerability scanner and has reduced performance limits. Burp Suite Professional, priced at $475 per user per year, adds the full automated web vulnerability Scanner that crawls and audits applications for issues such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting, unthrottled Intruder for faster automated attacks, access to the BApp Store for community-built extensions, and PortSwigger's newer Burp AI capabilities. Burp Suite Enterprise, marketed as Burp Suite DAST, is a separate CI/CD-oriented dynamic application security testing product designed for team-based, scheduled, and pipeline-integrated scanning; it is Docker-deployable, offers centralized vulnerability management and reporting across many applications, and is sold on a custom-quoted annual basis, with real-world enterprise contracts commonly landing in the tens of thousands of dollars per year depending on scan volume and support tier. Burp Suite is a desktop Java application that runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux. It is the de facto standard tool taught in many penetration testing and bug bounty training programs, and PortSwigger's free Web Security Academy (built around Burp) is widely used to learn web application security concepts such as SSRF, insecure deserialization, and access control vulnerabilities. Typical users are penetration testers, red teams, application security engineers, and bug bounty hunters who need both automated scanning and fine-grained manual request manipulation in one toolkit.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Burp Suite is a web application security testing toolkit developed by PortSwigger, founded in 2008 by Dafydd Stuttard and based in Knutsford, UK. It combines an intercepting proxy, automated vulnerability scanner, and a set of manual testing tools used by penetration testers, bug bounty hunters, and application security teams worldwide.

PortSwigger has grown from Stuttard's solo project into one of the leading vendors in web application security testing, with Burp Suite reportedly used by more than 80,000 individuals and over 1,000 enterprise customers, and the company received a $112 million growth investment in 2024.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Burp Suite's toolset centers on an intercepting proxy that lets testers view and modify traffic between browser and server, Repeater for manually resending and tweaking individual requests, and Intruder for automating customized attacks such as parameter fuzzing. Professional and Enterprise editions add an automated Scanner that crawls applications and audits for common vulnerability classes.

Additional utilities include Decoder and Comparer for data encoding and diffing tasks, Sequencer for analyzing the randomness of session tokens, and the BApp Store, a marketplace of community-built extensions that expand Burp's functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Burp Suite Community Edition is free but excludes the automated scanner and throttles Intruder speed. Burp Suite Professional costs $475 per user per year and unlocks the full scanner, unthrottled Intruder, BApp Store access, and Burp AI features.

Burp Suite Enterprise (marketed as Burp Suite DAST) is a separate CI/CD scanning product for teams, priced on request; real-world enterprise deals are commonly reported in the tens of thousands of dollars annually depending on scan frequency and support requirements.

features.title: Intercepting proxy
features.description: Captures and lets testers inspect or modify HTTP/S and WebSocket traffic passing between the browser and target application.

features.title: Automated vulnerability scanner
features.description: Crawls application content and actively audits it for vulnerability classes such as SQL injection and XSS (Professional and Enterprise editions).

features.title: Repeater
features.description: Lets testers manually resend and modify individual requests repeatedly to probe for input-based vulnerabilities.

features.title: Intruder
features.description: Automates customized attacks by inserting payloads into defined positions across repeated requests, useful for fuzzing and brute-force testing.

features.title: BApp Store
features.description: A marketplace of community-built extensions that add functionality on top of the core Burp toolset.

features.title: Sequencer, Decoder, Comparer
features.description: Utility tools for analyzing session token randomness, decoding/encoding data, and diffing two pieces of data or requests.

features.title: Burp Suite DAST (Enterprise)
features.description: A CI/CD-integrated, Docker-deployable dynamic scanning product for scheduled, team-managed scanning across many applications.

pros: Industry-standard toolkit taught extensively in penetration testing and bug bounty training
Free Community Edition provides real manual testing capability, not just a crippled demo
Deep manual testing controls (Repeater, Intruder) alongside automated scanning
Extensible via the BApp Store for custom or niche testing needs
cons: Full scanning capability requires the paid Professional license
Enterprise/DAST pricing is not public and requires a sales conversation
Steeper learning curve for testers unfamiliar with manual proxy-based workflows
Community Edition throttles Intruder speed, limiting large-scale automated testing

faqs.question: Is Burp Suite free?
faqs.answer: Burp Suite Community Edition is free and includes the core manual testing toolkit, but it lacks the automated vulnerability scanner found in Professional and Enterprise editions.

faqs.question: How much does Burp Suite Professional cost?
faqs.answer: Burp Suite Professional is priced at $475 per user per year.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Burp Suite Professional and Enterprise?
faqs.answer: Professional is a desktop application for manual and semi-automated testing by an individual tester. Enterprise (Burp Suite DAST) is a team-oriented, CI/CD-integrated scanning platform for scheduled scans across many applications.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Burp Suite run on?
faqs.answer: Burp Suite is a Java-based desktop application that runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

faqs.question: Who makes Burp Suite?
faqs.answer: Burp Suite is developed by PortSwigger, a UK company founded in 2008 by Dafydd Stuttard and headquartered in Knutsford, Cheshire.

use_cases.title: Manual web application penetration testing
use_cases.description: Security consultants use Burp's proxy, Repeater, and Intruder to manually probe applications for logic flaws and input-based vulnerabilities.

use_cases.title: Automated vulnerability scanning in CI/CD
use_cases.description: AppSec teams deploy Burp Suite DAST in build pipelines to catch vulnerabilities before deployment across many applications on a schedule.

use_cases.title: Bug bounty hunting
use_cases.description: Independent researchers use Burp Suite Professional's scanner and manual tools to find and report vulnerabilities in bug bounty programs.
use_cases.audience: Independent security researchers and bug bounty hunters

features: Manual testing tools only, no automated scanner

enrichment.short_description: Butter is an AI-native video creative suite that lets marketing teams build branded motion graphics, ads, and social videos from reusable templates.
enrichment.seo_title: Butter Review 2026: Video Creative Suite Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Butter is an AI-powered video creative suite for marketing teams. See features, templates, pricing, pros, cons, and FAQs before you sign up.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Butter (usebutter.com, app.butter.video) is an AI-native video creative suite founded in 2020 and headquartered in Los Angeles, California, led by CEO and co-founder Nate Yu, who previously worked on growth teams at Shyp and Athos and on the early team at Grubwithus (Y Combinator W12, which pivoted into GOAT). The company operates as a small, lean team - reported at around 10 to 15 people as of 2025 - and was generating an estimated $1.5 million in annual revenue as of mid-2025, according to third-party revenue-tracking sources. Butter's product lets marketing and creative teams produce branded videos, social ads, and motion graphics faster than traditional tools like Adobe After Effects by starting from a library of remixable templates built by real brands, then customizing them with a modular block-based editor rather than a blank timeline. The platform's stated customer base includes major brands such as Disney, SpaceX, DoorDash, Sony Music, and Lyft, and it markets itself specifically to direct-to-consumer (DTC) and e-commerce brands that need a high volume of short-form video content for paid social and marketing campaigns. Key product capabilities include a template and building-block library that teams can remix and save into their own on-brand libraries, motion graphics and animation tools designed to be more approachable than professional compositing software, and a set of AI-assisted features: automatic background removal for product and model footage, one-click AI video captioning with customizable caption styling, and automatic resizing (magic resize) of a single video into multiple aspect ratios for different social platforms. Note: there is a separate, unrelated company also called Butter (based in Denmark, founded around the same time, known for its virtual workshop and meeting-facilitation software with breakout rooms and a speaker queue) - the two products share a common name and, confusingly, some overlapping historical domain infrastructure, but public founder records (Nate Yu for the video product versus Jakob Knutzen, Christopher Holm-Hansen, and Adam Wan for the workshop product) indicate they are distinct ventures; this entry describes the video creative suite. Butter's pricing includes a free tier for individual use and paid Pro plans reported by third-party review sites to start around $18 per month, with limits based on factors like AI auto-captioning minutes; the company does not publish a full public pricing breakdown by feature, and a 7-day money-back guarantee applies to paid subscriptions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Butter is a video creative suite built for marketing and e-commerce teams that need to produce a high volume of branded video content - social ads, product videos, motion graphics - without a full video production team. Founded in 2020 and based in Los Angeles, the company is led by CEO Nate Yu and operates as a small, lean team.

Rather than starting from a blank editing timeline, Butter is built around remixable templates and reusable design blocks, so marketers can produce on-brand videos quickly and non-designers can still ship polished output. The company lists brands including Disney, SpaceX, DoorDash, Sony Music, and Lyft among its customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Butter's core workflow combines a growing template library with a modular, block-based design system, letting teams save their own branded templates and components for reuse across campaigns. Its motion graphics tools aim to offer After-Effects-style animation with a gentler learning curve.

AI-assisted features include automatic background removal for product and talent footage, one-click AI captioning with customizable styling, and automatic resizing of a single video into multiple social media aspect ratios.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Butter offers a free plan for individual use, with paid Pro plans reported by third-party sources to start around $18 per month; paid tiers include a fixed allotment of AI auto-captioning minutes and export options. Butter does not publish a detailed public pricing grid, and a 7-day money-back guarantee applies to paid subscriptions.

features.title: Remixable template library
features.description: A library of pre-built video templates from real brands that teams can duplicate and customize instead of starting from scratch.

features.title: Modular block-based editor
features.description: A component-based design system lets teams build and reuse branded elements across multiple videos and campaigns.

features.title: AI background removal
features.description: Automatically removes backgrounds from product and model footage without manual rotoscoping.

features.title: AI auto-captioning
features.description: Generates and styles video captions automatically, with a set number of captioning minutes included per plan.

features.title: Magic resize
features.description: Automatically reformats a single video into the aspect ratios needed for different social platforms.

features.title: Motion graphics tools
features.description: Provides animation and compositing capability aimed at teams who want After Effects-style output without the learning curve.

features.title: Team template libraries
features.description: Teams can save custom templates and building blocks so anyone on the team can produce on-brand video content.

pros: Template-first workflow speeds up video production for non-specialist marketers
AI captioning and background removal cut out manual, time-consuming editing steps
Free plan available to try the core editor before paying
Used by well-known consumer brands, suggesting production-grade output quality
cons: Full pricing tiers and feature-by-plan breakdown are not fully published
Small team size relative to larger video editing platforms
Name collision with an unrelated Danish company also called Butter can cause research confusion
Positioned mainly for marketing/social video, not general-purpose film or long-form editing

faqs.question: What is Butter used for?
faqs.answer: Butter is a video creative suite used by marketing and e-commerce teams to produce branded social videos, ads, and motion graphics quickly using templates and reusable design blocks.

faqs.question: Is Butter free?
faqs.answer: Butter offers a free plan for individual use; paid Pro plans are reported to start around $18 per month, though the company has not published a full public pricing grid.

faqs.question: Does Butter use AI?
faqs.answer: Yes. Butter includes AI-powered background removal, automatic video captioning, and automatic aspect-ratio resizing (magic resize).

faqs.question: Is Butter the same company as the Butter virtual workshop tool?
faqs.answer: No. The video creative suite (usebutter.com) is led by CEO Nate Yu and based in Los Angeles, while the separate virtual workshop/meeting platform also called Butter was founded in Denmark by a different founding team; the two share a name but are distinct companies.

faqs.question: Who is Butter for?
faqs.answer: Butter is primarily aimed at DTC and e-commerce brands and marketing teams that need to produce a high volume of short-form video content for social and paid media.

use_cases.title: Social ad production at scale
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use Butter's templates and magic resize to quickly produce multiple ad variants across social platforms.

use_cases.title: Branded template libraries
use_cases.description: Creative teams build a reusable library of on-brand templates and components so any team member can ship consistent video content.

use_cases.title: Product video creation
use_cases.description: Teams use AI background removal and motion graphics tools to turn raw product and lifestyle footage into polished marketing videos.

enrichment.short_description: ButterCMS is a headless content management system with an API-first architecture that lets marketers publish content while developers integrate it via API.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ButterCMS is a headless CMS with an API-first architecture. Compare pricing plans, features, pros, cons, and FAQs before choosing it.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ButterCMS is a headless content management system founded in 2014 in San Francisco by co-founders Abi Noda and Jake Lumetta, who built it after running into content-management pain points at a previous startup. In December 2022, ButterCMS was acquired by Tiugo Technologies, a company that operates a portfolio of content and localization SaaS products. ButterCMS separates content creation from content delivery: marketing and content teams write and manage content through a hosted admin dashboard with a WYSIWYG/visual editor and live preview, while developers pull that content into any front-end framework (React, Next.js, Vue, Rails, Django, and others) via a REST/GraphQL-style content API. This API-first design lets non-technical teams publish independently without needing a developer to push code changes, while still giving engineering teams full control over the presentation layer. Core features include a visual page builder and live preview, SEO metadata fields, a built-in AI Assistant for content generation and editing, role-based permissions, multi-locale content support on higher tiers, webhooks, a write API, and multi-environment support for staging and production content. Pricing runs on a tiered subscription model with five levels: a Free Developer plan (50K API calls/month, 100GB bandwidth, 5 pages, 50 blog posts) intended for personal, non-commercial projects; Basic at $71/month (100K API calls, 250GB bandwidth, 20 pages, 100 blog posts); Advanced at $224/month (500K API calls, 500GB bandwidth, 50 pages, 500 blog posts, optional add-ons like the write API and webhooks); Professional at $359/month, marketed as the 'best choice' tier (1M API calls, 1TB bandwidth, 100 pages, unlimited blog posts, 3 locales, multi-environment add-on); and a custom-priced Enterprise/Agency tier with unlimited limits, a dedicated customer success manager, an SLA, and implementation support. Every paid plan includes unlimited users and reusable content components, plus a 14-day free trial, and annual billing offers roughly a 10% discount over monthly pricing. ButterCMS is positioned primarily for marketing teams at SaaS companies and startups who want to manage a company blog, marketing pages, or knowledge base without waiting on developer deploys for every content change, while giving engineering teams a stable API to build against.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ButterCMS is a headless content management system founded in 2014 in San Francisco by Abi Noda and Jake Lumetta, and acquired by Tiugo Technologies in December 2022. It separates content authoring from the presentation layer, letting marketers publish through a hosted dashboard while developers pull content into any framework via an API.

The product is aimed primarily at marketing and content teams at SaaS companies who want to manage blogs, marketing pages, and knowledge bases without depending on a developer for every content update, while still giving engineering full control over how content is rendered.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ButterCMS includes a visual page builder with live preview, SEO metadata fields, role-based permissions, and a content API that works with React, Next.js, Vue, Rails, Django, and other frameworks. Higher tiers add multi-locale content, a write API, webhooks, and multi-environment support for staging versus production content.
The platform also includes an AI Assistant to help teams draft and edit content faster, and every plan - including the free Developer tier - supports unlimited users and reusable content components.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ButterCMS offers a free Developer plan for non-commercial projects, then four paid tiers: Basic ($71/month), Advanced ($224/month), Professional ($359/month), and a custom-priced Enterprise/Agency tier. All paid plans include a 14-day free trial and roughly 10% savings on annual billing.

features.title: Visual editor with live preview
features.description: Marketers can edit content in a WYSIWYG interface and preview exactly how it will render before publishing.

features.title: Content API for any framework
features.description: A REST-style API delivers content into React, Next.js, Vue, Rails, Django, and other front-end or back-end stacks.

features.title: AI Assistant
features.description: Built-in AI tooling helps content teams draft, edit, and optimize content within the CMS.

features.title: SEO metadata fields
features.description: Native fields for titles, descriptions, and other metadata help content teams manage on-page SEO without developer involvement.

features.title: Multi-locale content
features.description: Higher-tier plans support managing content in multiple languages/locales from a single workspace.

features.title: Roles and permissions
features.description: Role-based access control lets teams restrict who can edit or publish specific content types.

features.title: Webhooks and write API
features.description: Optional add-ons on Advanced and Professional plans let external systems trigger updates or push content into ButterCMS programmatically.

pros: API-first design lets marketers publish without waiting on developer deploys
Works with virtually any front-end framework via a content API
Free Developer plan available for testing and small non-commercial projects
Built-in AI Assistant and live preview speed up content workflows
cons: Paid plans get expensive quickly for teams needing higher API call or bandwidth limits
Multi-locale support is limited to higher-priced tiers
Enterprise pricing is not publicly listed and requires a sales conversation
Fewer front-end starter integrations than some larger headless CMS competitors

faqs.question: What is ButterCMS?
faqs.answer: ButterCMS is a headless content management system that lets marketing teams manage content through a dashboard while developers integrate it into any front-end via an API.

faqs.question: Does ButterCMS have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, ButterCMS offers a free Developer plan intended for personal, non-commercial projects, with paid plans starting at $71/month for commercial use.

faqs.question: Who owns ButterCMS?
faqs.answer: ButterCMS was founded in 2014 by Abi Noda and Jake Lumetta and was acquired by Tiugo Technologies in December 2022.

faqs.question: What frameworks does ButterCMS work with?
faqs.answer: ButterCMS provides a content API that integrates with React, Next.js, Vue, Ruby on Rails, Django, and most other front-end or back-end frameworks.

faqs.question: Does ButterCMS support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Multi-locale content management is available starting on the Professional plan, which includes support for 3 locales.

use_cases.title: Marketing blog and content management
use_cases.description: SaaS marketing teams use ButterCMS to publish and update a company blog without needing a developer for every post.

use_cases.title: Multi-site content delivery
use_cases.description: Engineering teams pull the same content into multiple front-end properties (web app, marketing site, mobile app) via the shared content API.

use_cases.title: Localized marketing pages
use_cases.description: Global companies use multi-locale support to manage translated marketing pages from a single ButterCMS workspace.

features: Optional write API, webhooks, video uploads
Custom API calls, bandwidth, pages, collections

enrichment.short_description: Buzzsprout is a podcast hosting platform offering directory distribution, analytics, transcription, and monetization tools for independent podcasters.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Buzzsprout is a top podcast hosting platform with directory distribution and analytics. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Buzzsprout is a podcast hosting and distribution platform founded in 2009 by co-founders Tom Rossi and Kevin Finn and headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. The company has grown into one of the most widely used independent podcast hosts, reported to have around 53 employees, roughly 3,300+ paying customers, and approximately $5.6 million in revenue as of 2024, based on third-party revenue tracking. Buzzsprout hosts audio (and optionally video) episode files, generates a standards-compliant RSS feed, and automatically submits that feed to major podcast directories including Apple Podcasts, Spotify, and Amazon Music, removing the manual submission work podcasters would otherwise have to do themselves. The platform provides advanced download and listener analytics, automatic AI-generated episode transcripts, a customizable podcast website and embeddable/social-sharing episode players, monetization tools through an ad network and listener support/subscription features, custom RSS feed options, team collaboration for shows with multiple contributors, and audio processing tools. A newer Audio + Video plan lets podcasters publish video episodes directly to Apple Podcasts at up to 1080p resolution from the same platform used for audio distribution. Buzzsprout's pricing is usage-based around monthly upload hours rather than storage: a free plan allows 2 hours of uploads per month with episodes hosted for 90 days; the Audio plan is $15/month (billed annually) for 72 upload hours per year and one podcast; the Audio + Video plan is $25/month (annual billing) for the same upload allowance plus video publishing; the Multi-Podcast plan is $30/month (annual billing) and covers 5-10 shows sharing a pool of 180 upload hours per year; and a $5/month Archive plan keeps an inactive show's episodes live without allowing new uploads. All paid plans include automatic transcripts, directory distribution, custom RSS feeds, and team collaboration features. Buzzsprout is positioned for independent podcasters, small podcast networks, and businesses running branded podcasts who want hosting, distribution, monetization, and analytics in one platform without managing their own media server or manually submitting to each directory.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Buzzsprout is a podcast hosting platform founded in 2009 by Tom Rossi and Kevin Finn, headquartered in Jacksonville, Florida. It handles the technical side of running a podcast - media hosting, RSS feed generation, and directory submission - so creators can focus on producing episodes rather than infrastructure.

The company has grown to roughly 53 employees and thousands of paying podcaster customers, and is commonly recommended to new podcasters for its straightforward setup process and built-in directory distribution to platforms like Apple Podcasts and Spotify.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include automatic submission to major podcast directories, advanced download and audience analytics, AI-generated episode transcripts, a customizable podcast website, and embeddable episode players for social sharing.

Buzzsprout also offers monetization tools through an ad marketplace and listener support/subscriptions, team collaboration for multi-host shows, and an Audio + Video plan that publishes video episodes to Apple Podcasts at up to 1080p.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Buzzsprout prices plans around monthly/annual upload hours rather than storage. The free plan allows 2 hours of uploads per month with 90-day hosting; paid plans start at $15/month (billed annually) for the Audio plan, scaling up to $25/month for Audio + Video and $30/month for the Multi-Podcast plan covering up to 10 shows. A $5/month Archive plan preserves an inactive show without new uploads.

features.title: Automatic directory distribution
features.description: Submits the podcast RSS feed automatically to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, and other major directories.

features.title: Advanced analytics
features.description: Provides detailed download and listener data, including geographic and platform breakdowns, beyond basic download counts.

features.title: AI-generated transcripts
features.description: Automatically transcribes episodes, improving accessibility and giving podcasters repurposable text content.

features.title: Podcast website and players
features.description: Includes a customizable podcast website plus embeddable and social-media-optimized episode players.

features.title: Monetization tools
features.description: Offers an ad network marketplace and listener support/subscription options to help podcasters generate revenue.

features.title: Video podcast publishing
features.description: The Audio + Video plan lets creators publish video episodes directly to Apple Podcasts at up to 1080p resolution.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Supports multiple team members and co-hosts working on the same show from one account.

pros: Simple setup with automatic submission to all major podcast directories
Free plan available to try the platform before paying
Built-in AI transcripts and analytics without needing third-party tools
Video podcast publishing supported alongside traditional audio hosting
cons: Free plan limits uploads to 2 hours/month with only 90-day episode retention
Pricing is based on upload hours, which can be confusing compared to flat unlimited-storage competitors
Advanced monetization features depend on meeting ad network eligibility requirements
Higher-tier plans required for shows publishing many hours of content per month

faqs.question: Is Buzzsprout free?
faqs.answer: Buzzsprout has a free plan that allows 2 hours of uploads per month, with episodes hosted for 90 days; paid plans start at $15/month for more upload hours and permanent hosting.

faqs.question: Does Buzzsprout submit my podcast to Apple Podcasts and Spotify?
faqs.answer: Yes, Buzzsprout automatically submits your podcast's RSS feed to major directories including Apple Podcasts, Spotify, and Amazon Music.

faqs.question: Does Buzzsprout offer transcripts?
faqs.answer: Yes, Buzzsprout automatically generates AI transcripts for episodes on paid plans.

faqs.question: Can I publish video podcasts on Buzzsprout?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Audio + Video plan lets you publish video episodes directly to Apple Podcasts at resolutions up to 1080p.

faqs.question: How is Buzzsprout pricing structured?
faqs.answer: Buzzsprout charges based on monthly or annual upload hours rather than total storage, with plans ranging from a free 2-hour/month tier up to a Multi-Podcast plan covering multiple shows.

use_cases.title: Independent podcast hosting
use_cases.description: Solo podcasters use Buzzsprout to host episodes, generate an RSS feed, and get automatically listed on major directories.

use_cases.title: Podcast networks with multiple shows
use_cases.description: Networks use the Multi-Podcast plan to host 5-10 shows under one account with a shared pool of upload hours.

use_cases.title: Branded business podcasts
use_cases.description: Companies use Buzzsprout's analytics and monetization tools to run a podcast as part of a content marketing strategy.

features: Video publishing to Apple Podcasts up to 1080p
Keeps podcast live without new uploads

enrichment.short_description: Bytebase is an open-source database DevOps tool providing schema migration, SQL review, and GitOps-based change management for teams running multiple databases.
enrichment.seo_title: Bytebase Review 2026: Open-Source Database DevOps Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bytebase is an open-source database CI/CD and schema change tool. See pricing, features, self-hosting options, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bytebase is an open-source database DevOps and CI/CD platform founded in 2021 by co-founders Tianzhou Chen (CEO) and Danny Xu (CTO), former technical leads on Google Cloud SQL and Google's API infrastructure. The company is headquartered in Mountain View, California, and operates with a small team of roughly a dozen employees. Bytebase's source code is publicly available on GitHub under the bytebase/bytebase repository and the project is listed in the CNCF and Platform Engineering landscapes, positioning it as the 'GitHub/GitLab for database DevSecOps.' The tool addresses the database change management lifecycle - schema migrations, data changes, SQL review, version control of schema changes, and rollback - for teams that would otherwise coordinate these changes manually or through ad hoc scripts. Bytebase supports a broad set of database engines including PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, TiDB, Snowflake, ClickHouse, MongoDB, Redis, Oracle, SQL Server, and Google Cloud Spanner. Key capabilities include native GitHub/GitLab integration for a database-as-code (GitOps) workflow where schema changes are reviewed and merged like application code, automated schema migration with built-in rollback support, more than 200 configurable SQL lint rules for enforcing review standards, column-level data masking for protecting sensitive fields, and fine-grained role-based access control at the project and workspace level. Bytebase follows an open-core pricing model with three tiers. The Community edition is free forever and self-hosted, supporting up to 20 users and 10 database instances with community support and core database change management. The Pro plan costs $20 per user per month, is cloud-hosted (though self-hosting is also supported), removes the user cap, adds Google/GitHub SSO, user groups, and database groups, with email support. The Enterprise tier is custom-priced on an annual contract and adds advanced authentication (OIDC, LDAP, SCIM, 2FA), custom approval workflows, dynamic data masking, unlimited audit log retention, custom roles/branding, and optional add-ons like high availability and HIPAA BAA support, payable via credit card, wire transfer, or AWS/GCP Marketplace. Because it is open source and self-hostable at the Community tier, Bytebase is commonly adopted by engineering teams, DBAs, and platform engineering groups who want database schema change control (similar in spirit to Flyway or Liquibase, but with a web UI and governance workflow) without being locked into a single vendor's cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Bytebase is an open-source database DevOps platform founded in 2021 by Tianzhou Chen and Danny Xu, former Google Cloud SQL engineers, and headquartered in Mountain View, California. It manages the database schema and data change lifecycle - migrations, SQL review, version control, and rollback - across a wide range of database engines.

The project is open source on GitHub and listed in the CNCF landscape, and it can be fully self-hosted for free at the Community tier, making it a common choice for engineering and platform teams that want database change governance without vendor lock-in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bytebase provides GitOps-style database-as-code workflows via native GitHub/GitLab integration, automated schema migrations with rollback, and more than 200 configurable SQL lint rules for review standards. It supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, TiDB, Snowflake, ClickHouse, MongoDB, Redis, Oracle, SQL Server, and Spanner.

Enterprise-focused capabilities include column-level dynamic data masking, fine-grained role-based access control, advanced authentication (OIDC, LDAP, SCIM, 2FA), and audit logging for compliance-sensitive environments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community edition is free forever, self-hosted, and supports up to 20 users and 10 database instances. The Pro plan is $20 per user per month and removes the user cap while adding SSO and group management. Enterprise pricing is custom and billed annually, adding advanced compliance and support features.

features.title: GitOps database-as-code workflow
features.description: Integrates natively with GitHub and GitLab so schema changes go through the same review and merge process as application code.

features.title: Automated schema migration with rollback
features.description: Applies database schema changes automatically and supports rolling back a change if something goes wrong.

features.title: SQL review with 200+ lint rules
features.description: Enforces SQL style and safety standards automatically before changes are applied.

features.title: Broad database engine support
features.description: Works across PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, TiDB, Snowflake, ClickHouse, MongoDB, Redis, Oracle, SQL Server, and Spanner.

features.title: Dynamic data masking
features.description: Masks sensitive column-level data for users without appropriate access, available on Enterprise.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Provides fine-grained permissions at the project and workspace level for teams and DBAs.

features.title: Self-hosted or cloud deployment
features.description: Can run fully self-hosted for free at the Community tier or as a managed cloud service on paid plans.

pros: Free, self-hostable Community edition with real database change management functionality
Open-source codebase avoids vendor lock-in
Wide database engine support in a single tool
Built by former Google Cloud SQL engineers with deep database infrastructure experience
cons: Community edition caps users (20) and database instances (10)
Enterprise pricing is not publicly listed and requires an annual contract
Small company/team size relative to established database tooling vendors
Some advanced governance features (masking, custom approval) reserved for paid tiers

faqs.question: Is Bytebase open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bytebase is open source and available on GitHub, with a free, self-hostable Community edition.

faqs.question: What databases does Bytebase support?
faqs.answer: Bytebase supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, TiDB, Snowflake, ClickHouse, MongoDB, Redis, Oracle, SQL Server, Spanner, and more.

faqs.question: How much does Bytebase Pro cost?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan is $20 per user per month and can be self-hosted or used as a managed cloud service.

faqs.question: Who founded Bytebase?
faqs.answer: Bytebase was founded in 2021 by Tianzhou Chen and Danny Xu, former technical leads on Google Cloud SQL, and is headquartered in Mountain View, California.

faqs.question: Does Bytebase support GitOps workflows?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bytebase integrates natively with GitHub and GitLab so database schema changes can be reviewed and merged like application code.

use_cases.title: Database schema change management
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use Bytebase to review, approve, and apply schema migrations safely with rollback support.

use_cases.title: SQL review and governance
use_cases.description: Platform teams enforce SQL lint rules and approval workflows before changes reach production databases.

use_cases.title: Compliance-sensitive data access
use_cases.description: Organizations use dynamic data masking and RBAC to restrict sensitive data access across regulated environments.
use_cases.audience: Enterprises in finance, healthcare, and regulated industries

enrichment.short_description: Cabin is a lightweight, privacy-first, cookieless web analytics tool that tracks visitors without cookies, IP storage, or fingerprinting.
enrichment.seo_title: Cabin Analytics Review 2026: Pricing & Privacy Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cabin is a privacy-first, cookieless Google Analytics alternative. See pricing, features, compliance details, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cabin (withcabin.com) is a lightweight, privacy-first web analytics tool positioned as a cookieless, carbon-conscious alternative to Google Analytics. The product was created by developer Nic Mulvaney and was originally incubated inside Normally, a digital product design studio, starting around 2020. When Normally closed in January 2025, Cabin became a fully independent product wholly owned and operated by Mulvaney, making it effectively a solo-run indie tool rather than a venture-backed company. Cabin's core technical differentiator is that its tracking script does not use cookies, does not store visitor IP addresses, and does not perform browser fingerprinting; instead it uses a method based on comparing a resource's last-modified time against the start of the day to estimate unique visits without persisting any personal identifiers. This design is intended to make Cabin compliant with GDPR, CCPA, ePrivacy, and PECR by default, meaning sites using Cabin generally do not need to show a cookie consent banner. All data is described as end-to-end encrypted and processed and stored exclusively within the EU, and the service states it runs on 100% renewable energy. In addition to standard analytics (page views, referrers, geographic and device breakdowns, custom events, UTM campaign tracking), Cabin includes a distinctive carbon-emissions reporting feature that estimates the CO2 footprint of a website's traffic over time and highlights oversized page assets contributing to that footprint. The company also publishes a public analytics API. Cabin's script is marketed as roughly 77 times smaller than Google Analytics' tracking script, which the company says collectively saves users terabytes of data transfer each month across its customer base. Pricing is simple and two-tiered: a free Basic plan covers one website with 30-day data retention, unlimited page views, data export, CO2 reporting, custom events, weekly email reports, and a custom subdomain for the public dashboard; the Pro plan is $19 per month and removes the website cap and retention limit, offering unlimited websites and unlimited data retention with the same feature set, subject to a fair-use policy. Cabin is aimed at privacy-conscious website owners, indie developers, and small businesses in the EU (or serving EU visitors) who want basic traffic analytics without a cookie banner or the compliance overhead of Google Analytics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cabin is a privacy-first, cookieless web analytics tool created by developer Nic Mulvaney, originally incubated at the design studio Normally starting around 2020 and now run independently since Normally closed in January 2025. It positions itself as a lightweight, ethical alternative to Google Analytics.

Rather than using cookies, IP address storage, or browser fingerprinting to identify visitors, Cabin uses a method that estimates unique visits without storing any personal identifiers, which the company says makes sites using it compliant with GDPR, CCPA, ePrivacy, and PECR without needing a cookie consent banner.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cabin provides standard analytics - page views, referrers, geographic and device data, custom events, and UTM campaign tracking - through a script reported to be about 77 times smaller than Google Analytics' tracking code. All data processing and storage is described as EU-based and end-to-end encrypted.

A distinctive feature is Cabin's carbon-emissions reporting, which estimates a website's CO2 footprint from traffic over time and flags oversized assets contributing to it. Cabin also offers a public analytics API and weekly email reports.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cabin has two plans: a free Basic plan covering one website with 30-day data retention and unlimited page views, and a $19/month Pro plan that removes the website cap and retention limit for unlimited websites and unlimited data retention (subject to fair use).

features.title: Cookieless visitor tracking
features.description: Estimates unique visitors without cookies, IP address storage, or browser fingerprinting, avoiding the need for a cookie consent banner.

features.title: EU-based, encrypted data processing
features.description: All analytics data is processed and stored exclusively in the EU and described as end-to-end encrypted.

features.title: Carbon emissions reporting
features.description: Estimates the CO2 footprint generated by a website's traffic and flags oversized page assets.

features.title: Lightweight tracking script
features.description: The tracking script is reported to be roughly 77 times smaller than Google Analytics, reducing page load overhead.

features.title: Custom events and UTM tracking
features.description: Supports tracking custom user actions and campaign attribution via UTM parameters.

features.title: Public analytics API
features.description: Provides an API for pulling analytics data programmatically into other tools or dashboards.

features.title: Weekly email reports
features.description: Sends automated weekly summaries of site traffic to site owners.

pros: No cookie consent banner needed due to cookieless, non-identifying tracking design
GDPR, CCPA, ePrivacy, and PECR compliant by design with EU-only data storage
Very lightweight script compared to Google Analytics
Unique carbon footprint reporting not found in most analytics tools
cons: Free plan limited to a single website with 30-day data retention
Run as a solo/independent project, which may raise longevity or support-capacity questions for larger teams
Feature set is more basic than full-featured analytics suites like GA4 or Plausible
Pro plan's 'unlimited' page views are subject to an undefined fair-use policy

faqs.question: Is Cabin free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cabin has a free Basic plan for one website with 30-day data retention; the Pro plan is $19/month for unlimited websites and unlimited data retention.

faqs.question: Does Cabin use cookies?
faqs.answer: No, Cabin does not use cookies, store visitor IP addresses, or perform browser fingerprinting, which generally removes the need for a cookie consent banner.

faqs.question: Is Cabin GDPR compliant?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cabin is designed to be GDPR, CCPA, ePrivacy, and PECR compliant by default, with data processed and stored exclusively in the EU.

faqs.question: Who runs Cabin?
faqs.answer: Cabin was created by developer Nic Mulvaney, originally incubated at the design studio Normally around 2020, and has been fully independent since Normally closed in January 2025.

faqs.question: Does Cabin track carbon emissions?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cabin includes a feature that estimates a website's CO2 footprint from its traffic and highlights large assets contributing to it.

use_cases.title: Cookie-banner-free analytics
use_cases.description: Site owners who want to avoid displaying a cookie consent banner use Cabin's non-identifying tracking method.
use_cases.audience: Privacy-conscious website owners and indie developers

use_cases.title: GDPR-compliant EU analytics
use_cases.description: Businesses serving EU visitors use Cabin's EU-only data storage and compliance-by-design approach to simplify privacy compliance.
use_cases.audience: Small businesses and startups serving EU users

use_cases.title: Sustainability-focused reporting
use_cases.description: Teams tracking their website's environmental impact use Cabin's carbon emissions reporting alongside standard traffic metrics.

enrichment.short_description: cal is the classic Unix command-line utility that prints a simple text calendar for a given month or year, built into virtually every Unix-like operating system.
enrichment.seo_title: cal Command: Unix Calendar Utility Guide & Usage
enrichment.seo_meta_description: cal is the built-in Unix/Linux/macOS command-line calendar tool. Learn its history, usage, options, and how it differs from ncal and gcal.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: cal is not a company or SaaS product but a small command-line utility for printing a text-based calendar, present in essentially every Unix and Unix-like operating system since the earliest days of Unix. The original version was written by Doug McIlroy (also known for inventing the Unix pipe) for the first PDP-11 implementation of Unix around 1971, and a cal command was present in Version 6 AT&T UNIX, making it one of the small set of utilities included in Ken Thompson's original Unix implementation. Because of this lineage, cal is specified in the Single UNIX Specification and ships by default on Linux distributions, macOS and other BSD-derived systems, Plan 9, and Inferno, typically as part of a base utilities package (on Linux, often bundled in packages like util-linux or bsdmainutils depending on distribution). With no arguments, cal prints a calendar for the current month to the terminal; it also accepts a month and year to print an arbitrary month, or a single year argument to print all twelve months of that year at once. Common options let users control whether the week starts on Sunday or Monday, display Julian (day-of-year) dates alongside calendar dates, and highlight the current day. A well-known quirk of cal, dating back to its earliest Unix versions, is its correct handling of the September 1752 calendar transition, when eleven days were dropped from the Julian-to-Gregorian calendar switch in Britain and its colonies - running 'cal 9 1752' on many implementations shows this historical gap. Because cal is a small, static utility rather than an evolving product, it has no company, no pricing, no roadmap, no accounts, and no version-specific feature tiers in the way a commercial SaaS tool would; different operating systems bundle slightly different implementations (for example, GNU coreutils/util-linux's cal on Linux versus BSD's cal on macOS), which can produce small differences in default output formatting or supported flags, but the core function - printing a calendar to a terminal - has remained consistent for more than five decades. Related and complementary tools include ncal (an alternate output format available on most systems) and gcal (a more feature-rich GNU calendar utility that is a separate, optional install). Because cal ships as a standard part of the operating system rather than being installed or purchased separately, it is best understood as a ubiquitous, zero-configuration utility for developers and system administrators who need a quick calendar reference inside a terminal session, rather than as a commercial product with a company behind it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: cal is a command-line utility that prints a plain-text calendar to the terminal. It is not a commercial product or company; it is one of the original small utilities from early Unix, written by Doug McIlroy for the first PDP-11 Unix implementation around 1971 and present in Version 6 AT&T UNIX.

Because of that lineage, cal is specified in the Single UNIX Specification and ships by default with virtually every Unix-like operating system, including Linux distributions, macOS, and other BSD systems - there is nothing to install, sign up for, or pay for.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: With no arguments, cal prints the current month; it also accepts a month and year to display an arbitrary month, or a single year to print all twelve months at once. Common flags control whether weeks start on Sunday or Monday and whether Julian (day-of-year) dates are shown.

A frequently cited historical detail is cal's handling of the September 1752 Julian-to-Gregorian calendar switch, when eleven days were dropped from the calendar in Britain and its colonies - many implementations still reproduce this gap when run against that specific month and year.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: cal is free and included by default with the operating system; there is no license, subscription, or paid tier. Related tools like ncal and the more feature-rich GNU gcal are separate, optional utilities on some systems.

features.title: Current month display
features.description: Running cal with no arguments prints a calendar for the current month by default.

features.title: Arbitrary month/year lookup
features.description: Accepts a month and year argument to display the calendar for any specific month in history or the future.

features.title: Full-year view
features.description: Passing a single year argument prints all twelve months of that year at once.

features.title: Configurable week start
features.description: Supports flags to start the displayed week on either Sunday or Monday.

features.title: Julian date display
features.description: Can optionally show day-of-year (Julian) dates alongside standard calendar dates.

features.title: Historical calendar accuracy
features.description: Correctly reflects the September 1752 Julian-to-Gregorian calendar transition on many implementations.

pros: Available by default on virtually every Unix-like system with no installation needed
Extremely lightweight and fast for quick terminal reference
Simple, stable command syntax that has changed little in decades
No cost, license, or account required
cons: Output formatting and supported flags vary slightly between implementations (GNU/Linux vs BSD/macOS)
No graphical interface, reminders, or event/scheduling functionality
Limited to displaying dates - no integration with calendar apps or scheduling systems
Less commonly used since GUI and web-based calendar apps became standard for scheduling

faqs.question: What does the cal command do?
faqs.answer: cal prints a plain-text calendar to the terminal, showing the current month by default or any specified month or year.

faqs.question: Is cal free?
faqs.answer: Yes, cal is a free, built-in command-line utility included by default with Unix-like operating systems; there is no cost or license involved.

faqs.question: How do I show a whole year with cal?
faqs.answer: Running cal followed by just a year (e.g. cal 2026) prints all twelve months of that year.

faqs.question: Does cal work the same on macOS and Linux?
faqs.answer: The core functionality is the same, but macOS/BSD and Linux (GNU/util-linux) implementations can differ slightly in default formatting and supported flags.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cal and ncal?
faqs.answer: ncal is a related utility, available on most of the same systems, that displays the calendar in an alternate vertical layout compared to cal's default horizontal grid.

use_cases.title: Quick terminal date reference
use_cases.description: Developers and sysadmins run cal to quickly check a date or day-of-week without leaving the terminal.

use_cases.title: Scripting and shell automation
use_cases.description: Shell scripts parse cal's output or use its date logic as a lightweight way to reference calendar information.
